can't úteosure!" I caU bullshit on that, too, tharks toJim roto-rooting it out of my
head. It's okay to measure your sales, clicks, opens, etc. But at the end of the day you
don't really have all üat much contol over them. And obsessing over them like way too
ma¡y email maxketers do is counterproductive in my humble, but accurate. opinion. And,
yes, inJim Camp's opinion, too. In his book "NO" he spends an entie chapter on üe
futiüty of üe whole idea of sa.les quotas and outcome dependence. Why? Because you
have no ¡eal control over those things. And, thus, they make you, by default, needy. Aul
the needier you are. üe less lüely you a¡e ing (.this goes for
marketing,lop) driting. selling. negotiath$ . One of
the most liberating teachings I ever done heard was from Sean D'Souza during his talk at
the 2008 System Seminar. The guy does very very well. And he said (paraphrased):
"We don't use analytics a¡d I don't have any data - I startéd out as a cartoonist and I
moved to marketing and thi§ has a¡lowed us to také 3 vacations per year, buy houses,
tnvel, do a.ll the things we really warted to do.IMe earn more money tha¡ wé need."
Contrast that to the blurry-eyed Internet marketer constandy frealing out (and
maling his entire team miserable) because they didn't get as mar-ry opens or sa.les as üey
should have on Tuesday or whatever I ain't saying not to measure. Personally I
'?neasure" my sales trends. Not daily sales. But monthly arrd yeariy Are they trending up
o¡ down? Sometimes they trend down. Do I freal< out about it? No. I locus on the one
thing I eao control day in, and day out Writing better emails and copy Doing üings that
bond me more to my list. Creaúng better offers. Alrd so on, and so forth. I can't cont¡ol
my sa.les and neither ca-n you. But you can control your acúvities "doing the right
things long enough consistently" as the old school sa-les trainers used to say This goes
beyond business, too. OnJaauary 1st 2016 elBenbo weighed in at a fat 184 pounds. (X4y
normal weight is üe mid 150's, give or ta"ke.) Instead of setting a specific weight goal
(which wouldjust stress me out each morning on the scale as my weight fluctuated
weighing yourself is perhaps the dumbest and most f¡ustmting way to measure weight loss
because your body retains water and is char-rging constandy and marry úmes upticks in
weight may have nothing at all to do wiü fat and you could even be technically
üinner...) I focused on what I cor¡ld control. Like eating better, exercising, not drinling
(as much, hch) beer or eating sweets, upping my protein inta-ke, walking more, dúnking
more wate! not going out to eat as much, and so on. Those üings I can control. Hitting a
specific weight by a specific date I really ca¡r't. A¡d so it is. Hellz tlis applies even in the
dating world. rr{¡hen most people get dumped they start frea}ing out about how drey will
find a new bofriend or girlfriend. Wrong thing to do. If you're a guy, forget trying to get

heard. Finally he tossed the script away, lnocked on üe dooq and said, "I don't know if I

People would let him in and ask to tell them morc. Women in üat neighborhood had
terribly dry and nasq, hair and they told him all about it, creating a vision of it, and when
he pitched his water filter it was a no-brainer In fact,Jim_sakl hotadcraote_money

The O y rSafe Place" You'Il Ever Need
One of üe most profound things I heardJim Camp say is, 'you're always safe in
üe other person's world". As long as you are in gq!¡ world, dealing üth Sg!¡ vision and
problems and desires, you cannot really bore them. For example, Iast year my dad had a
bad mole problem in his yard. We're talking 30+ mole hills. There was nothing you could
say to him about moles that would not have been 10070 interesting to him. So it is in your
business. This goes back to knowing your market, of course. The safest place you can be
in marketing and selling is their world - üeir vision, not your üsion, assumptions, or
super duper product. Trying to pull them into your world is doing it the hard way I am
not saying it doesn't work, but it's a helluva lot harder in my e4terience. True story:
I,\¡hen I was doing client work I had a chat with a potentia.l client who wa¡ted me to wdte
a sa.les letter. At the end of tüe ca-ll I nonchalantly asked, "how are your guys' emails
doing?" Bam. I got an earful about how crappy their r.esponse and sales were, how they
were getting lots of spam complaints, lots oI opt outs, not neaxly as marry sales as üey
wanted, etc. I was in üeir world. After üat, I didn't even have to say anltJring He asked,
'l\try, can you do emails too?" It's a.ll about bcing in their world - in your emails, sales
copy negotiations, face-to-face selling phone selling videos, blog posts, articles, and all
your marketing
The Best Way To Get A Yes, Is To Get Thern Telling You No
This may sound contraria-n (again) but tIe goal in all my selling is never to get a
yes. It's to get a "no" instead. This cor¡ld be an entire newsletter issue in itself But let me
give you an example. When my ex-copy'lr,riting apprentice and I broke up last summer I
was back on the chase again pretty qückly And, at the time, I was readingJim Camp's
book about trying to get people to say no first. So, I decided to test it out on some chicks I
wa-nted to date. I would literally try to get them to tell me no to dates, hanging out, etc.
Did it work? Ridiculously well. I remember telling one girl who was being kind of üshy-
